title: Trees Produce Food
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

required.title: [Activity] Access to fruit trees or berry bushes
[Activity] A tree or shrub to plant

suggested.title: [Video] Time-lapse videos of fruit trees through seasons

competence_markers.description: The child has harvested fruit or berries from a perennial plant and eaten it
The child has participated in planting at least one fruit tree, berry bush, or vine
The child can explain that perennial plants produce food for many years, unlike annual garden crops
The child has observed pruning and can explain in basic terms why it helps the tree
The child has observed a perennial plant through at least two seasons and can describe how it changes
The child understands that planting a tree is an investment in the future — a delayed gratification concept

content.why_this_matters:
Most of the food in a grocery store was planted this year and harvested this year. Lettuce, tomatoes, beans, corn — they all grow from seeds, produce food, and die within a single season. But some of the best food comes from plants that live for decades: apple trees, cherry trees, blueberry bushes, grape vines, walnut trees. These perennial plants produce food year after year, getting bigger and more productive over time.

When someone plants an apple tree, they are making a promise to the future. That tree will not produce fruit for several years. But once it starts, it may produce apples for fifty years or more. Your great-grandparents may have planted trees that are still producing food today. And if you plant a tree now, your grandchildren may eat from it.

This is a different kind of food growing than planting a garden. It takes patience. It takes faith in the future. And it teaches you something important: some of the most valuable things in life take years to grow.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Understanding that trees and shrubs produce food** — connecting the fruit on the table to the tree in the yard
2. **The experience of planting a perennial** — participating in an investment that takes years to produce
3. **Basic pruning observation** — understanding that cutting a tree can help it grow better
4. **Seasonal awareness** — observing a perennial plant through a full year of dormancy, growth, flowering, fruiting, and rest
5. **Harvesting from perennial plants** — picking fruit, berries, or nuts from established plants
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Food That Comes Back Every Year

Your garden produces food for one season. When frost comes, the tomato plants die. Next year, you start over from seed. But fruit trees and berry bushes are different. They go dormant in winter — they rest, drop their leaves, and wait. When spring comes, they wake up and produce food again. And again. And again.

An apple tree can live and produce fruit for 50–100 years. A grape vine can produce for even longer. A walnut tree planted today may still be producing nuts two hundred years from now. These are plants that outlive the people who plant them.

**What perennial food plants include:**
- **Fruit trees:** Apple, pear, plum, cherry, peach, apricot
- **Nut trees:** Walnut, hazelnut, chestnut, pecan, almond
- **Berry bushes:** Blueberry, raspberry, blackberry, currant, gooseberry
- **Vines:** Grape, kiwi, hardy passionfruit
- **Other:** Strawberry (technically perennial), rhubarb, asparagus

### Part 2: Planting a Tree — A Gift to the Future

When you plant a fruit tree, you will not eat from it right away. Most fruit trees take 3–7 years before they produce their first real harvest. This is hard. You do the work now and wait years for the reward.

But here is what makes it worth it: once that tree starts producing, it produces for decades. The person who plants an apple tree at age seven may still be eating apples from that tree at age sixty. And so may their children.

**How to plant a tree:**
1. Choose a sunny spot with good drainage. Fruit trees need at least 6–8 hours of direct sun.
2. Dig a hole twice as wide as the root ball but no deeper. The tree should sit at the same level it grew in the nursery.
3. Place the tree in the hole. If it is grafted (most fruit trees are), make sure the graft union — the bump near the base of the trunk where one variety was joined to another — stays above the soil line.
4. Fill the hole with the soil you removed. Press it down gently to eliminate air pockets.
5. Water deeply — several gallons for a new tree.
6. Spread mulch around the tree, but keep it 6 inches away from the trunk. Mulch piled against the trunk invites rot and pests.
7. Water regularly through the first year while the roots establish.

Your tree. Your responsibility. Check on it. Water it in dry weather. Watch it grow.

### Part 3: Pruning — Cutting to Help

It seems strange that cutting parts off a tree would help it grow better. But pruning is one of the most important things you do for a fruit tree. Here is why:

- **Light:** A tree with too many branches shades its own inner parts. Fruit needs sunlight to ripen. Pruning opens the tree so light reaches all the branches.
- **Air:** Dense branches trap moisture, which encourages disease. Pruning lets air circulate through the tree.
- **Shape:** An unpruned tree grows in whatever direction it wants. A pruned tree grows in a shape that is strong, balanced, and easy to harvest from.
- **Energy:** A tree puts energy into every branch. If you remove a weak or badly placed branch, the tree redirects that energy into the branches you keep — making them stronger and more productive.

At your age, you will watch pruning, not do it yourself (pruning tools are sharp and the decisions are complex). Watch an adult prune a fruit tree. Ask them to explain each cut: "Why are you cutting that branch? What will happen?" Notice that every cut has a reason.

### Part 4: A Tree Through the Seasons

Choose one fruit tree, berry bush, or vine. Observe it through a full year:

**Winter:** The tree is bare. It looks dead, but it is not. Inside the buds — small, tight packages on every branch — the tree has already formed next year's leaves and flowers. The tree is resting, conserving energy for spring.

**Spring:** Buds swell and open. Leaves unfurl. Flowers appear — and those flowers, if pollinated by bees, will become fruit. This is the most critical and most fragile time. A late frost during bloom can destroy an entire year's crop in one night.

**Summer:** Fruit develops and grows. The tree puts enormous energy into filling its fruit with sugars, acids, and nutrients. The leaves are working hard, photosynthesizing — turning sunlight into food.

**Fall:** Fruit ripens and is harvested. Leaves change color and fall. The tree begins to shut down for winter, pulling nutrients from its leaves back into its roots and wood for storage. This is not death — it is preparation.

And then the cycle begins again.

### Part 5: Harvesting from Perennial Plants

Picking fruit from a tree you planted — or from a tree someone else planted years ago — is one of the most satisfying experiences in food production. The tree did most of the work. You tended it, and now it gives back.

**How to harvest:**
- Pick fruit when it is ripe. For most tree fruits, this means it comes away from the branch with a gentle twist — not a hard pull.
- Handle fruit gently. Bruises rot quickly.
- For berries, pick when fully colored and slightly soft. Taste one — if it is sweet, they are ready.
- Harvest in the morning when fruit is cool.
- Some fruit (apples, pears) can be stored for months in cool conditions. Others (peaches, berries) must be eaten or preserved within days.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Harvest Experience
**Developing well:** The child has picked fruit from a tree and can describe the experience. They understand that the fruit came from a plant that was there last year and will be there next year.

**Developing slowly:** The child has not had the opportunity to harvest from a perennial plant.

**Diagnostic questions:** If access to fruit trees is the barrier, visit a u-pick farm or orchard. Even a single visit satisfies this requirement.

### 2. Planting Participation
**Developing well:** The child helped dig the hole, place the tree, fill the soil, and water. They know where their tree is and check on it. They understand it will take years to produce fruit.

**Developing slowly:** The child watched an adult plant a tree but did not participate.

### 3. Seasonal Observation
**Developing well:** The child has observed a perennial plant in at least two distinct seasons and can describe the changes. Their observation journal (if maintained) shows visual or written documentation.

**Developing slowly:** The child knows trees lose leaves in fall but has not made systematic observations.
